_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve graphs Treasury yields across maturities from 1 month to 30 years, where a normal upward slope indicates higher yields for longer maturities reflecting healthy economic expectations. An inverted curve, characterized by short-term yields exceeding long-term yields, typically precedes recessions within 6-18 months. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.48% for June 2026, with the 10-year minus 2-year (2s10s) spread at 0.35 percentage points, indicating a positively sloped curve. This contrasts with the 2022-2024 period when the 2s10s spread was inverted, down to as low as 0.0 points—the deepest inversion in this series—before recently re-steepening above zero.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread serves as a key recession signal; an inversion below zero has reliably preceded every modern US recession. The curve’s return to a positive slope from the deepest inversion around zero points to a shift in market expectations. However, the New York Fed’s recession probability model currently assigns a 31% chance of recession, suggesting moderate risk. Importantly, the yield curve often un-inverts shortly before an actual recession begins, so the present positive slope does not eliminate near-term recession risk.
notes.now: Currently, the yield curve’s positive slope and the 0.35-point 2s10s spread suggest diminished immediate recession risk compared to the previous inverted period. The 31% NY Fed probability reflects non-negligible but not elevated recession odds. Collectively, the curve signals caution without definitive near-term recession onset indications.
